title: Sentence and Paragraph Motions
subtitle: {key:(} {key:)} — sentences. {key:{} {key:}} — paragraphs.
summary: ( and ) jump to the previous and next sentence. { and } jump by paragraph (blank-line separated).

blocks.text: When you're navigating prose, words and lines are too small. Vim offers two larger units: sentences (delimited by `.`/`!`/`?`) and paragraphs (delimited by blank lines).

These pair with operators just like every other motion. {key:d}{key:}{key:}} deletes from the cursor to the next blank line. {key:c}{key:)}-type-replacement-{key:Esc} changes the rest of the current sentence.

blocks.title: What Counts as a Sentence / Paragraph
blocks.text:
**Sentence** boundary: `.`, `!`, or `?`, optionally followed by closing brackets/quotes ({key:)}, {key:]}, {key:"}, {key:'}), then whitespace or end-of-line.

**Paragraph** boundary: a blank line. The `'paragraphs'` option also lets nroff-style macros count, but that's vintage stuff.

In code, paragraph motion is *surprisingly* useful: function bodies are usually surrounded by blank lines, so {key:}{key:}} jumps function-by-function in well-formatted code. Sentence motion is less useful in code because `.` appears in method calls, but in markdown it's perfect.

blocks.title: Sentences and paragraphs are pattern-defined
blocks.text: {key:(}, {key:)}, {key:\{}, {key:\}} aren't AST-aware — they scan for delimiters: a sentence ends at `.`, `!`, or `?` followed by spaces or end-of-line; a paragraph boundary is a blank line. Both motions are character-class scans, the same machinery as word motions but with different separators. That's why prose with non-standard punctuation sometimes confuses them.
